So this is the final call, it's the fifth call, and we were scheduled for four calls and then we added this one. So this is a bonus call. This is extra. You didn't pay for this. So if it's great, I'm sure you'll love it. And if we suck, remember you didn't pay for it and you can't yell at us because it's free. But we won't suck and Clayton never does. Now Clayton, before I start to talk to you, you would remind me, you said, Bob, last week you ended the call saying I should talk about, I promised to talk about the two things that I learned from you that I thought were most immediately useful to me. So let me say what they are. Number one, Clayton puts more of an emphasis on building credibility very early than most other copywriters I've seen. And he does this, you look at the packages, most of which are tabloids and magulogs. And you notice that most magulogs and tabloids you get just start with a headline. So you have five ways to grow richer as interest rates rise. But Clayton's pieces all have what I call a credibility pre-head or some people call it an eyebrow. There's a line of copy and smaller type above the headline, not a subhead under it, but there's one above it and it's almost always dedicated to credibility. Like on this piece I'm reading which Clayton, I think you wrote this for wise five ways to grow richer as interest rates rise. That's right. Yes, this is the top. Martin D. Wise, PhD, who warned of today's rising interest rates 10 months in advance reveals dot, dot, dot and then it goes into five ways to grow richer as interest rates rise. So it's to build credibility earlier than other people do with a pre-head. And the other interesting idea that you gave when you were talking about, I think I'm under arrest here, can anybody hear that? Yeah. I gotta go guys. Watch that, will you? Yes. So the second thing was this idea you have of, I always thought when you write a promotion what do you sell? You sell the product. But you've said several times that with a long copy promotion like a tabloid or a maglong, anything that 16 to 20 pages are over, you actually have to sell the promo first. You have to first sell the reader on reading the promo, that reading this is good for you and useful and you'll get something out of it before you can go into selling the product. And that's something that I found very useful in both of those things. I've just made, these things are only really valuable if you listen to them and use them. And those are two things, not just the only two things that I took out of what you gave and have been using since you started this series. So those are my favorites. Now in this call, let me just, I don't think people have seen the agenda because we added this call as a bonus. You promised Clayton sent me an agenda and his agenda says he's going to tell us in the next hour. Now remember, these calls are an hour but we asked you guys listening to leave 90 minutes. So we're going to get through, do the first part, take about an hour. We're going to do call number five which he's titled 37 Proven Secrets Guaranteed to get your big winners more often. These are power copywriting techniques. We'll do that in the hour. And then whatever time remaining in that 90 minutes, we'll take your questions. And Julie, we're still active with the server and getting emails. Yes, yes. So they can send questions to feedback at nakesythtotalpackage.com. And please, like I said in the past, put teleconference question in the subject line so I can recognize it immediately. And we will try to get to it later, later in the call. Now I have some questions we've already received. But if you haven't sent them or you want, your field of question hasn't been answered, resend it and I would do that now. So looking at this agenda, which just sounds terrific, Clayton has promised us for today, selecting the best themes for your promotions. And that was actually a question from one of our listeners, Dede, who said, how do you do that? How do you select the best format for a mailing? In other words, should you make it a magologue or a tabloid? How to write a great opening, your lead. Then he's got an item, the number one blunder that even A-list writers make. So I want to see what that is. Side, because he didn't say. Sidebar savvy. We're going to go over, we had in the outline we already gave you, we listed a bunch of sidebars, but he's going to go over that in detail so you know how to use them. How to make your guarantee sell like crazy instead of just making it boil or play text as so many people do. Writing closing copy that prospects can't say no to. And I think a lot of writers also treat the closing. Clayton, see if you disagree or agree, because you mentor a lot of writers. A lot of people kind of cheat and treat the closing as boilerplate. They just lift it for what was done before and they don't give it as much thought as the rest of the package. And then how to navigate the response device minefield. Some really good tips here on how to make order forms that really work. And that also makes a difference. It's really a mistake to treat the, I think the order form, the closing copy, the guarantee, all the standard stuff as boilerplate. And here Clayton is going to show us how not to do that. So Clayton, thank you and welcome to call number five. Thanks a lot. So how do you, let's get right into it. How do you select the best, what are the themes for promotions? What are the major types of themes? And how do you select, how do you know how to select the best one? Well, before I get to that, let me just preface what we're going to talk about today by saying that, you know, this entire teleconference series has been about building a more profitable or an extremely profitable copywriting business. And we've talked about all of the things that you have to do as a copywriter to attract top clients. We've talked about billing. We've talked about the deals you make. We've talked about contracts. We've talked about the nuts and bolts of the business of being a copywriter. But the obviously the number one thing that you can do to take your copywriting business to the moon is to create bigger winners more often. If you forget everything else you've learned on this series, but you go out there today and you write a package for a major client or any client that blows the doors off anything else that he's ever done or that anyone in his industry has done, then your name is going to get around very quickly and your dance card is going to be very full. And you're not going to have to worry about raising your rates because your clients will do it for you. As I've said so many times, you don't have to raise your rates. All you have to do is write winners and then be unavailable when somebody calls you to try to get on your dance card and they will raise your rates for you. So today's call, we're going to be looking at copywriting techniques. Some of them we've covered briefly before, but we're going to try to get in more depth for you today. As far as the theme, selecting a theme is concerned. It's really an appropriate question right now. I just returned from meetings in the Washington, DC area with a major client. And we spent two days focusing on two of that client's newsletter franchises. And during those meetings, we spent 100% of our time simply talking about what the product was. And I think this is an important first step, whether you're meeting with a client or whether you're sitting in your office at home and beginning the writing process. Step number one really is what do I have to sell? And taking a look at that, in this case, it was a newsletter editor, a doctor. And we talked about who he is, how the impression, the physical impression that he gives, his training, his qualifications, his background, and what it is that he has a heart for in terms of what he wants to do for his readers and by extension for his patients. And we had to start with that. I always say start with a prospect, but this is really a preliminary step. We didn't write headlines. We didn't list. We didn't look at the product itself. We just basically said, you know, what is the positioning of this particular doctor? And again, please forgive me. Some of my examples are going to be in areas that you're not working in yet, but I think if you think about them, you will find application in the areas you are working in. And we found this one doctor, for example, who is a naturopath. He's a very good looking man. He's in his 50s, but he's matured very gracefully. He is there. He lives in the Los Angeles area. He has a lot of movie stars and film people who come to him. And he has a very holistic approach to health and to reversing disease. And so we sat around and talked about how we really wanted to position the guy. And we decided that this particular guy, that he is basically a walking testimonial for aging without getting old. And we decided that that is going to be the kind of tableau against which we write all of his packages for the next six months. And so I then came back and I went through his material and I asked myself, how is, first of all, I said, who's our prospect? And my answer in this particular industry is it's typically, they'll tell you, it's 60%, 40% male, 40% female. But one thing that people don't think about is a lot of those guys on the file, are really their spouses. A promotion piece came to the household. The man may or may not have subscribed, but in many cases, it was addressed to the man, but the woman in the household ended up buying the product. And so, and that's true on a lot of the lists that we're going to, especially in the health area. So the mix is probably closer to 50-50 or maybe even it's, it could be skewed towards the female side and there's no real way to know that. So it's equally important to consider how men think about themselves as they age and how women think about themselves as they age. And so I went through a long list of thoughts and of course, I'm 53, so it was very easy for me to write the male side of it. What do I think about aging? What do I think about myself in this situation? And these are just thoughts, beliefs, and so forth about this topic. Then I went in and I said, how do I feel about each one of those thoughts? Remember, we talked about the fact that thoughts trigger emotions. And so I asked myself, how do I feel about each one of these thoughts? Am I afraid? Am I disgusted? Am I frustrated? Am I excited? You know, what are my feelings? And then I did the same thing for the women in our audience. What are they thinking about aging? And I found, and I wasn't able to answer that question. I sat here and stared at my computer screen and finally said, what the heck am I trying to answer this for? You know, I don't know anything about what it's like to be a 50-something-year-old woman. So, or a woman who's aging for that matter. So I called my good friend Carleen. And it just turns out that Carleen had had a kind of a focus group at her house where she'd invited a bunch of women in our prospect market at each range to her home. And if Kim's on the call, Kim, I think you were probably there. I know some people from various publishers around DC were there. And so she had both people in our prospect group, but also copywriters and marketing people from the health industry there. And they sat around and they had coffee and tea and cookies and talked about what it was like to be a woman seeing yourself growing older. And so I just asked her the question one time. And I couldn't get a word in edgewise for about 90 minutes. And she just did a brain dump for me. It was wonderful. So I was able then to, instead of figuring it out on my own, I was able to go ahead and address those issues of what prospect is thinking about the topic we've chosen, the general topic area, and how she must be feeling about this issue of growing older. So that was step one. And I called out my springboard document because it's not headlines. It's not even themes yet. It just prepares us. We've connected our product to our market. And now it prepares us to start thinking about what the specific themes for the promotion pieces we're going to do are. Right. And when we do that kind of thing, basically there are four or five different kinds of themes that are, that kind of jump out of, because they're proven to work over time. And the first one is topical. And we've talked about that in the past. I'm going to kind of stick with this model of a health newsletter promotion. Sure. But so on a topical theme, what you do is you go to a library or you go to a website where you can access an archive of Time Magazine, Newsweek Magazine, the Pop Magazine. And also look at magazines like Prevention and other health-oriented magazines. And you go back over the last six months to a year. And you just kind of scanning covers. And you're looking for recurring themes that are topical. And it could be about, recently for example in the health industry, there's been a spate of research on the half of all heart attack victims who don't have clogged arteries. They over 400,000 a year die with healthy arteries. Well, why is that? Harvard University and an institution in London came up with the answer. And it was published in the Journal of the American Medical Association. And the answer is arterial inflammation that can be caused by something as innocent as a gum infection. And it causes a small piece of plaque to break loose and then triggers a heart attack or stroke. And so there's been a lot of reporting on that. That means it's in the forefront of our prospects minds. And it means that they have feelings about that. Okay? And the feeling is, holy cow, how do I make sure that I don't have inflammation in my arteries? And fortunately, there are answers to that. In the financial area, it's the easiest thing in the world because there's so much news and so many large long-term trends in the economy and also in the investment markets that it's fairly easy to identify them. And you don't necessarily even have to go online and look at all of these, you know, see what people are writing about them. We know that oil prices are five times higher than they were just a few years ago. And we know that gasoline prices are probably 50% higher than they were just a short time ago. And we know that interest rates are going up. And this has been a long-term trend and is likely to continue. And we know that inflation is increasing and that that's likely to be a long-term trend due to these gigantic government deficits. We know that China is coming on strong with a GDP growth in double digits now, almost three times ours. We know India is coming on strong. That's obvious fodder for a major thing. So those are all topical. And again, topicality sells because your prospect already knows about the trend. He already has feelings about it in the financial area. It's a sense of urgency. This thing is happening. Is it too late for me to get in? How do I profit from it? And he just has a sense of urgency. He wants guides. So topical theme is always a great way to go. Let me ask you a question in health, but especially in finance. Can you make the, can you conversely conclude that if copy is pretty devoid of topicality? And sometimes you see that where it seems like it could have been written now as easily as 10 years ago. Would that be a negative because the prospect would think, hey, these people do not know what's going on? Absolutely. And fortunately, topicality isn't the only theme you can use. So if you don't have, there are two traps here. Okay. The first trap is you pick something that's in the news and that you believe will be in the news for a year or more because you want your package to work for a year or more. Trap number one is the trend reverses. Okay. Right. And you might have a kick butt control that's mailing big numbers, but then somebody comes out with a piece of research that says, oh, never mind. Inflammation does not cause heart attacks. Or somebody, or the president suddenly cuts the budget and balances it. Inflation drops to zero. Interest rates fall. And you're out there in the mail with a promotion about rising interest rates being locked into the system. So that's trap number one. And it just illustrates why choosing your theme, if you're going a topical route, it's very important to select something that you have a 90 or 99% chance that it will continue for the foreseeable future. The other trap with this is picking a theme that is overused in the mail or that, as Bob says, is not particularly new news. It may be it's a long-term trend, but it's not at the top of the headlines. And people are not necessarily thinking about it. Or your lead comes off as just kind of feeling stale. If you can't come up with a hot topical theme that you feel will be in the news or continuing for some period of time, then there are some other ways you can go. So what would be some other themes I could use? Well, I was just talking about the spring board document that I did. Right. Where we talked about what our prospect is thinking and feeling relative to the subject area we've selected for our doctor to specialize in for a health newsletter. So another way to go would be what I call a prospect-driven piece. And it's essentially a lead where you connect with the strongest emotion that you've been able to identify in the prospect relative to the general tableau we've created. And the example I used earlier was on this thing about aging without growing old. Right. So the idea there is to say, okay, we've identified all of the strongest emotions that we've been able to that our prospects have about this. And, you know, we looked at how the prospect feels about sex relative to growing older. How the prospect feels about physical performance, his or her physical performance, ability to work and play as they grow older. We talked about their own sense of self-value. What are the things that they draw value from in their life? Let me figure a better way to say that. Each of us thinks of ourselves as having value for very specific reasons. And for men it's- Me because I'm very good looking for example. That's the- yeah, drop dead gorgeous and a snappy dresser, yeah. And so Bob, of course, it's obvious where he draws his value from. And- but men tend to draw our value from the fact that we're good wage earners, good providers from the fact that we- good competitors, physical competitors that, you know, we could kick anyone's butt in the bar and we're more athletic maybe. And many of us draw value from that even when we're old and fat. That doesn't tend to change. We still see ourselves as 18 years old. We draw- women tend to draw their sense of value from- okay. I'm going to give some stereotypes. You're going to get in trouble, but go ahead. Yes, but remember we are in the business of stereotypes. When we select a mailing list we are saying these prospects are stereotypically this age group, this sex, this income and so forth. And so in our business we recognize the stereotypes by the very nature are never 100% accurate, but there's enough truth in them that we can mind them in some cases. When we're convinced that there's some truth in the stereotype. But many of the women in our audience draw a lot of self-worth from the fact that they are nurturers. And they feel that they have tremendous value because they take care of their spouse or they take care of their children or grandchildren or they bake them cookies. They may not be totally taking care of them, but they're doing things for them that are kind of nurturing. And if a woman gets ill, if she gets older, she's unable to do that. And a major part of her value as a woman is stolen from her. And she's going to have very strong feelings about that. And so the prospect driven lead will essentially identify the dominant resident emotion that you feel is strongest among your prospects. We'll call that person out and put their feelings into words in a way that we'll have them saying, this is for me, and get them nodding their head right away. And then offer a solution. That's the prospect driven lead. You're not talking about the product. You're not talking about the value of what you're going to give them free right up front necessarily. You're not talking about benefits off the top. They come in the deck copy. You still need to get them on page one, but it's not the main lead. Instead, you're going to a prospect and you're giving him a slug in the gut by talking to him about something that he feels is deepest, most powerful, most compelling, fears, frustrations and desires. Do you remember John Francis Tiedel, the co-writer? No. So he had written a famous package. And it was actually, I can't remember the publication, but I remember the headline was, and this was 20 years ago. I don't think it worked today, but it was the same thing. It was, are you sick and tired of feeling sick and tired? Right. Yep. Absolutely. That is a perfect example of the prospect driven emotion, our dominant emotion headline. My goodness, time is going. Let's move on. The next one is the one that every AWAI student knows by heart, and that's the benefit driven headline. And that's just simply where you present your benefit in the headline. There's nothing wrong with that. I found that it's second best for me because, as we've discussed before, people, benefits tend to connect with your intellect. They tend to rationalize, give you a reason why you should buy a product. And 99% of our purchase decisions are not made on a rational basis, but instead on an emotional basis and to fill in emotional needs. Therefore, the benefit, I personally, my opinion is that the benefit driven headline tends to be weaker than the prospect driven lead. However, again, on my lead, which I define as my first one to three pages, but especially on page one, I make sure I include benefits. But the benefits that I include tend to have more to do with a sale, a reason why you should read this than they do about my product or my product. You're selling the promotion, not the product. That's right. So the benefits I use on my cover tend to have to do with, you know, if you read this, I'll make sure you don't have a heart attack, a stroke, or I'll show you how to make a million dollars investing in rising oil prices. Right. So the benefit is there. The next one is intrigue driven. This is to me actually stronger than a benefit driven and especially in the financial markets. A few years ago, I did a lead that was the greatest medical breakthrough since the discovery of penicillin. And that's a perfect example. John Tide, this guy I mentioned, he wrote a package for psychology today, which was a very successful control. And it was, I don't know if you remember this. Do you close the bathroom door even when you're the only one home? Yeah, I have. I love it. And it's just intrigue. Depending on how well you connect with where your prospect is, intrigue driven can be very strong. In the financial markets, especially, Bill Bonner is famous for calling someone else who says, and somebody at his company and saying, I got a great idea for a package. The headline is man who bought Microsoft at $5 a share reveals the stock he's buying now. You know, well, you know, at the time before the stock split, Microsoft was over $100 a share. They obviously this guy made 20 to one on his money. Everyone wants to know the answer to that. Now, Bill didn't have an answer. He just had a great headline idea. And he said, it's you guys's problem to go find, you know, that guy and find the answer. But this is a great promotional idea. And I forget who it was. It could have been either John Ford or Michael Masterson. But one of them then wrote the package and it was a grand slam home run. But anyway, that's kind of the intrigue driven thing works great in health works great in financial. And so long as you know what it is your prospects want, I told a client the other day, look, you know, just tell me what your prospect fears, is frustrated with or desires. And I'll give you a 4% control. To the degree that you're accurate, if you're 100% accurate, we'll have a 4% control. If you're 50% accurate, we'll probably get a 2% control. But you give me that information and then I can write an intrigue driven headline that will absolutely call those guys out on those feelings. Or I'll write a prospect driven benefit or prospect driven dominant emotion headline that will call them out. Or you can do a topical. Or you could do a topical on that. And then benefit driven is not your favorite choice. What else is there? The other way, and I don't see this very often and it's an opportunity that I'm going to fully exploit. And that is all of our clients have testimonials about their products. And they have the names and addresses of people who have sent those testimonials. Why not have a testimonial driven lead? Why not find the strongest testimonial, two or three strongest testimonials that your client has, and sit down and call those people and talk to them on the phone and ask them how they felt before their problem was solved, before they met the client and before they bought the product. How did the problem, this product solve, reduce the quality of their life? Can I give you a great example of that? You know Peggy Sarnoff? Yes. She wrote a package. I'll probably, if she's listening, I'll probably misquote it because I don't have it in front of me for a financial newsletter. And the Adirond blobs said something like, quote, I can't believe it, end quote. The voice on the other end of the phone told me, I followed your advice, did your trade, and in one day I made $325,000 end quote. Right. It was a testimony. Wonderful. And you could move on. You could go two ways from there. You could go immediately to how this happened. In other words, the engine that caused this problem to happen. In other words, it was my unique approach to investment that did it. Or it was this one little secret that gives you an edge over 99% of all other investors. Or it was a particular type of investment that did it. The other way to do it is to go from that headline and then go deeper into the story. Right. You know, her electricity was about to be shut off. She'd been investing for years and never had a winner. You know, she'd seen it all before, heard it all before, tried all these other advisors. Nothing worked. She was skeptical when she heard about this approach. She thought, what the heck, she'd give it a try. And imagine you could have knocked her over with a feather when she got her brokerage statement. And just imagine how it felt to go down to the bank and hand that teller a check for $100,000 and say, put it in my account. Just really help the prospect vicariously experience what the product is going to do for them. You can spend a page or two if you're really writing this in a way that is engaging and fascinating and the prospect is saying, that's me. I can see myself doing this. And you've got a very strong then testimonial driven lead. And I've even gone so far then afterwards to get photographs of the new house that was purchased, the new car that was purchased, the family reunion that was paid for by this money. But you can only derive those things by going the extra mile and calling the person on the phone and finding out how they were before the solution arrived, how they felt about the solution when it was presented to them, and what happened and then how much better their life is specifically and what ways since using the solution. Okay, so you can dig into a testimonial which as you point out is not as commonly used as other approaches or a story. Then the question arises, so you've given us choice of themes and again, they're just sum up. There's a topical theme, there's the prospect driven theme, but based on their dominant emotion of feeling, there's the benefit driven theme which usually derives from the product, you know, it stops hemorrhoids or whatever. There's the intrigue driven, why close the bathroom door when you're the only one home, there's the testimonial or the story. You can select from those themes, but how do you select the format for your direct mail promotion? Do you do that? Does the client do that? How does that work? Typically, I'll do it and essentially when choosing format, you have, you know, you can go with an envelope package or a component package that'll have, you know, a sales letter, a lift letter, a response device, possibly a flyer in it. You can go with a digest format package which is typically 8.5 by 11. It's also called a book-a-log, 8.5 by 11 page size and they can run 48, 64 pages long, typically with a four color cover and two color insides. You can go with a special report format which is 8.5 by 11, toned down, very serious looking, very credible looking. You know, not with all of, you know, the guys jumping on the beach on the cover and all of the stock photos. We've seen a million times in Magalox. You can go with Magalox or you can go with tabloids. Magalox look more like magazines, tabloids. I call them the biggest, fattest, happiest, flusy in the biker bar. They are huge, they're colorful, there's eye candy everywhere you look. Essentially, you have two decisions to make. The first one is who am I talking to? If you're talking to very serious people, investors, people who are ill, or if you're addressing a subject that people are very serious about, it could be something as trivial as stamp collecting, but the people that are into it are, I mean, this is serious. This is their life. They love it. Then you're going to want a format that sends the message that we're serious too. If on the other hand you have a product that appeals to a more consumer marketplace, like for example, a very general health publication that has to do with avoiding disease. That's going to a very broad market. Then a more consumer-ish look is a great way to go. That would be more like a magaloc or the tabloid. With boardrooms, tabloids work extremely well because they're going to a very broad marketplace. Their prospect is anybody who has a checking account on their financial side. On their health side, it's anyone who has a body. They have extremely huge universe and extremely diverse types of mailing lists they go to. Those promotions, and they're talking about subjects that can be fun, whatever to eat on an airplane. Bill's okay to pay late. Bill's okay to pay a day late. How to get Broadway tickets free. Stuff like that. That's wonderful, intriguing stuff. It's fun stuff. There are a lot of visual images that you can put with that to even strengthen the copy. Then you go with a much more colorful format. Hang on just a second. The message, just keep in mind though, the tone of the message is reflected, either strengthened or weakened by the format that you choose. Then again, let's talk about just a couple of other considerations here. If you're going up against a strong control, a good rule of thumb is to do exactly the same format as the control. That way if you should unfortunately lose, you know it's not that you picked the wrong format. Well, yeah, the way I look at it is not so much I'm worried about losing. I'm more concerned that if I write a stronger copy, which of course is my intention, than the control, I don't want to lose because my package costs more money. If I'm writing against a strong control, I don't want to lose because the format I chose limited me to less copy space than the control. Got it. So it's very important to me in 99% of all cases to look at the control. If it's a weird looking 20 page tabloid with real floppy, not very thick, clients are going to get a weird looking 20 page tabloid from me. Once I have the control, and this just happened last week, once I have the control, I will immediately go to the client and say, okay, well I hated that format in the first place, but since we have a control, I want to spin off a digest version of it, or a book log, or a special report version. I have a very strong control out there for a supplement product. I suggested to the client several months ago that as soon as we established it as a control, we immediately spin off a digest format. And we're around our doubling date right now, and the digest is out pulling the 24 page tabloid by about 50%. And the control isn't old enough to begin tiring yet, so that's a big lift in response for him. But on my first outing, I went up against his control, which was a tabloid, and I gave him exactly the same number of pages, so my piece cost exactly the same as a control. So cost is a very important factor, and consider that seriously. This format question used to be the hardest thing for me when I first started writing a pack. As soon as I sat down and really thought about the numbers, I never had to worry about what the format was. For all the reasons you said you really thought it through, and I think most people say, well I look at the control and I want to pick some really weird or different format, because that will be the difference. But you're pointing out why that's not good thinking, and for the reasons you just stated, the most sensible thing is to duplicate or use the same format, but write stronger copy. That's right. I'd like to just say one other thing. If the control is an envelope package containing several components, and especially if the envelope is oversized, it's important for copywriters to understand that jumbo envelopes and other odd-shaped envelopes are custom jobs. That limits the number of times your client can go into the mail with it, because there's quite often a six to even eight week fabrication period required for these odd-size envelopes. If you go with that format, you're basically limiting the number of mailings your client can do with your new control over the next year. That's not smart. My one caveat would be if the control is a component package, that's the one time that I would consider breaking that rule and going with the self-mailer instead. They're much quicker to produce, and the client can produce more mailings per year and make you greater royalties as a result. Smart advice. I want to keep you on track. The next topic I want to ask you about, you've written this package, let's say it's a tab, a word, or a magologue. Now you're looking at your copy. How do you do the due diligence to look at your headline and say, yeah, that's the best it could be? How do you rate your headline? I've been thinking about just doing an article on this or even putting up a web page so that everybody could just simply go in and click a button and give every headline they write a rating of one to five on the basis of 11 questions and then get a grade for how strong that headline is. There are several things that I look for in a headline. Number one is pretty elementary and that's the big miss. The number one purpose of a headline is to get attention. I've often said that the best headline in the world is sneaking up behind somebody with a cap gun and pulling the trigger. You have their attention. A headline doesn't have to present every benefit in the world. It doesn't have to do anything really but grab attention. You can do all of the other things that you need to do in your eyebrow or pre-head or in your deck copy and of course in the opening copy of the actual piece. So size, envision your prospect at the mailbox. You just open the thing up, especially this time of year. What do you got? He's got 27 catalogs. Every single one of them is 8 and a half by 11 format. He's got his bills. He's got letters from friends and relatives. He's got all of this other stuff in the mailbox and somewhere in that stack of mail is your piece. Your entire future is hanging. Number one, size matters in terms of the headline. Your piece will not be read if you don't get his attention. You won't get his attention unless you catch his eye. So go with bigness. Number two is resonance. How well does this headline resonate with my reader? Example, do you make these mistakes in English? Yes. I think the resonance factor, if you do the other things we've talked about in terms of connecting, identifying what he's thinking about himself relative to the subject and how he then feels about those things, I think you will resonate with him. I think he'll see this and say, this is for me. Resonance is basically calling out your prospect and letting him know this is for him. Second thing is a benefit either implicit or explicit. And again, how strong is the benefit you're offering? It doesn't matter. Of course, as I said, I prefer on my cover to provide the benefits for reading this because I'm asking this guy, look, turn off the TV, tell your wife to leave you alone for five minutes, tell the kids to leave you alone for five minutes so you can read this because this is the most important thing in your mailbox today. And here's why. So I want his full attention and the way to do that is to sell him on reading this. And so the benefits of reading this piece to me are the key on the outside. Sell the promotion first, not the product. Yeah, if you're writing shorter copy, of course, great. Go straight for the benefits of your product. Then after making, asking about, well, is my leave, my headline, I really mean the pre-headline deck. I think, Rob, we do have someone who wants to know, the question is very simple, what do you mean by a deck? He doesn't know that term. Okay, deck is the enlarged copy below the headline, but before the actual text cut, the running text begins in your promotion. Some people might call it a subhead. Yeah, to me a subhead is more, you know, a small headline breaking up columns of text. Right. But this deck copy is smaller than the headline, it's bigger than the running text, and it's in between them. Yeah, and it tends to expand upon the headline and then present the benefits for reading this or the benefits of the product. After you've done that, and you can still do this in the deck, or you can actually begin moving into your text with it, does it present a horrifying alternative? Now, both the benefits and the horrifying alternative in this deck copy, the benefits should be explicit. The horrifying alternative could be implicit or explicit. You might actually say, I mean, Jim Rutz once did a headline that said, read this or die. I mean, that presents you with two very clear choices. Right. And the horrifying alternative is right there in the headline. But this contrast, you know, between hot and cold, between benefits, you know, where you'll go if you read this and where you'll stay or go if you don't read this, that contrast is extremely powerful. So does your lead include a horrifying alternative? If you have a solution for arthritis, then, you know, what is the alternative to that solution? You know, being crippled for the rest of your life, you know? So, you know, think about whether or not you've adequately presented, you know, both sides of the coin. Now, on the other hand, some packages that you'll do will be fear-based packages, and so they'll lead with the fear, the horrifying alternative, and then in that case, that's fine. But follow it up with the benefit copy, the reasons why you need to read this. Got it. The next one is, does, is my lead emotionally driven? Does it connect with how my prospect feels? Does it expand on those feelings? Does it fully engage him emotionally? A lot of writers don't do that. A lot of writers, especially younger writers who have been taught to focus on benefits, will go into the text and just flatly state the benefits and move through the copy. And it comes across as kind of dry. And if your subject matter is, you know, if you're talking about heart disease and you're talking as if it were, as if this was some kind of a PhD treatise or something, then, you know, it doesn't work because it sounds like the speaker, the person who's writing these words, is a cold fish. And it just doesn't work on that level. It can also undermine credibility. So make sure that you have infused this early copy with emotion and that it's appropriate for the subject at hand. The next one we talked about is it topical? Now, again, all of the themes that we chose earlier that we talked about earlier, you can tell, you can see it's not a Chinese menu where it's, you know, you have to pick one. This is a mix and match thing. You can have something that's topical and emotional and benefit-driven. And I think the strongest leads combine all of this. So if you've chosen a theme that isn't, or a headline that is a particular topical, look for ways to bring in a topical element into the deck, into the opening copy. And even if it's, let's say, you know, you're talking about, you know, bankruptcy, right? And so you're appealing directly to the prospect's fear of bankruptcy or his desire to get out of debt. Working in early into the copy, some news story from Newsweek or Time, a three-journal about the fact that 1.5 million Americans will go bankrupt this year. Maybe even showing it, you know, reproducing the article brings in this topical feel that this is breaking news. This is something I need to know. Specificity is the next question. Do I have enough specifics in my headline and lead structure to make this credible? It's one thing to say that the person writing to use the smartest person who ever lived on this subject in the history of the universe since the Big Benning period. That is a claim. It's not necessarily a proven fact. But if, after having said that, you then demonstrate that this guy knows his stuff by including specifics about the subject at hand. Maybe it's the number of people who will have a heart attack with healthy arteries this year. Maybe it's how many medical studies have proven this supplement to be safe and effective. Maybe it's the name of the doctor who originally discovered CoQ-10 back in 1940, whatever, or 50, whatever, or EDTA in 46 or 45, I think. But have the specifics. We had a lead for one of our packages that I did for Weiss where I was able to say 346 of the S&P 500 companies are fudging their books. People looked at that and they said, man, this guy knows, you know, it's not 350. It was an odd number. So it was specific. I see people all the time using headlines like, you know, grow 10,000% richer or how I made so-and-so grow 500% richer. Well, nobody grows 500% richer. They grow 473.2% richer. So being more specific with the numbers and demonstrating your knowledge of the subject in the lead copy brings enormous credibility. So rate your headline on that. Again, we've talked about the readership sale. You've got to ask yourself, have I adequately sold the prospect on reading what follows? Have I grabbed him by the eyeballs, forced him down into the text, and made it, you know, it's like looking at a train wreck in slow motion only on the good side. Is it impossible for him to look away at this point? And if not, what can I do to strengthen that desire to read on? You know, the old thing about cliffhangers in the movie, you watch a 15-minute serial on one Saturday and it'll lift you so excited about seeing what happens next. You would wait a whole week and come back to the theater the next Saturday to see whether the guy fell off a cliff or not. The readership sale is absolutely essential. Then is there an element of irony or intrigue? People love this whole idea that something that helps you in this way hurts you in that way, or something that you've been told about a particular subject is now being proven wrong, or even if it's right, it also causes a problem in this other area. Any irony you can bring to the discussion, and this tends to be usually in deck copy or opening copy, sometimes in headlines. But this idea of irony is particularly powerful. It's a real key to me to a successful headliner lead, and it's not always easy to do, it's not always possible to do. But when you hit on some fact that you're going to reveal that the reader will be a real head scratcher, real ironic, or could be just intriguing, then you've got something, but the irony thing. I know Peter Belowski, who is an in-house guy for Phillips for years, and I don't think he was even writing copy at first for them. I think he was in marketing. He eventually talked them into letting him write a promotion piece, and it did very well, and he ended up going out on his own freelance. I think he's very good at this whole thing of finding ironies related to the subject at hand, and it hooks you in. It just works every single time for me. And then the final thing is, does my headline and deck structure present a proposition? And there are whole books written on this. If you haven't read the books that we've recommended, particularly Ogilvy, Rosser-Reeves, John Capels, I urge you to do so. And if you want a book list, write to us, and we'll get one to you, our recommended reading list. But there's been a lot written on this whole idea of a proposition, and the proposition can be in one of two general categories that particularly work well. The classic proposition is a way of asking a question or creating intrigue or irony in the prospect's mind. If doctors treat sick people all day long, why don't they get sick? Great example. There's a proposition. It's not saying, hey, I'm going to sell you something. It's something that stops you cold and say, yeah, how come? Another way of doing it, and that's particularly powerful. We could do an hour just on that. By the way, if any of you do not read Bence of Anga Bullets, do it. Subscribe now. As soon as you get off this call, it's benceofangabullets.com. The easing is free. Gary is a master of the proposition lead. He's a great guy, absolute genius. His easings are shorter, easy to read, and they're just extremely powerful. I strongly recommend that you read him if you're not already. He's a master of the proposition. Why don't we move forward? That's how to evaluate the headline. What about the headline that you said? If the headline does nothing else but get attention, it's successful. Now you've gotten attention. You've used as many of those 11 as you can. How do you now not lose steam and write a lead that now I say dear investor or dear reader? How do I write opening copy that hooks them and doesn't let them go? I want to move through these quickly. The first thing that I do, because we've covered some of this before, the first thing, now we're talking about you're actually in the text. You're in the beginning of the running text. You're after the salutation. That's right. The first thing is connect with the reader at the point of his need. You talk to the reader about the benefits you're going to bring to his life. This paragraph needs to connect with everything that went on before. You're following on the theme, but now you have an opportunity to expand on it even further. It's like the headline is the point of the nail that goes into the wood. The deck copy is a wider spot in the nail. Now you're just continuing to widen out, widen out. At this point now you're connecting back with that, but you're expanding. The first thing that I like to do is to immediately present a longer copy in the text. The reasons why receiving this particular communication is by far the most important thing that has happened to my prospect since he was born. I need to get him engaged and to understand that if he reads what follows, these wonderful things are going to happen in his life. If he doesn't, they won't happen and some terrible things might happen. That's presenting the horrifying alternatives. This is your opportunity to go into slightly longer copy about that. The next thing you do is you go back. We're repeating what we did on the cover or in our headline structure, but we're doing it in a different order and we're using longer copy to do it. Now you establish your credibility. In a financial package, my next step then is to say, not only am I telling you that you can do these things or that I will do these things for you, not only am I telling you that if you don't let me do these things, these bad things are going to happen, here's what I've done for other people just like you in the last 12 months. In a financial package, that's where I put my track record. I recommended oil stocks 12 months before oil took off. We made 1,000% on our money. I recommended interest rate vehicles just before interest rates started rising. We made 345% on our money. I did these things. The more specific, the better. That's your track record. If you don't have that kind of track record, like for example, in a supplement product, you won't have that kind of track record. This is a place to insert five quick testimonials. Each one of these testimonials is saying, you know what? I didn't think it worked either, but this worked for me. Here's what it did. My blood pressure dropped 20 points. My blood sugar dropped. My cholesterol dropped. They canceled my heart surgery. Those kinds of things. They're from real people who are testimonials. This is a proof element segment of the running text. Once you've done this, you don't necessarily have to go back to it if you do a good job on it. Although, I do use sidebars through the text to return to it, but not in the running text. Then, I tend to... Now, all of this, remember, is aimed at selling the reader on reading the entire piece. All of this should be accomplished in the first couple of pages of text at the most. I'm selling them on reading what follows. At this point, I'll say, spend a few minutes with me now, and here's what's going to happen. Bang, bang, bang. Maybe three, four, five things. Bullets, real quick. Then, I follow that immediately by saying something like, plus, I'll say something like, every fact and figure in this special bulletin today is taken from a volume that I've just... It's hot off the press, I've just completed, that I want to send to you free because this topic is so important. In a moment, I'll invite... The title of this volume is this, and in a moment, I'll invite you to send for it free. Then, at that point, you dive in. Now, it's time to fulfill everything that you've told them that you would tell them. This is the main body, the middle of the sales message is where you develop your theme. If you're talking about the product, you can begin by talking about empathizing, for example, with how terrible it is to have this problem, go straight to introducing the product, explain all of the things a product will do for the prospect, and then move into your premium sale or your offer. If it's an advertorial kind of piece where you're bringing value to the prospect's life in the piece, not just selling a product, which I highly recommend for most promotions, you dive into the reasons why, for example, in a financial package, why this trend is going to continue. If it's a health package, you talk about why it's a deadlock cinch that particular substances will reverse your heart disease or do whatever they're going to do. Those are some of the things that we should do right, but you've also promised us, and I'm reading from the notes you sent me, which I'm curious about, the number one blunder that even A-list writers make. What's the big mistake that people do that screws all this up? There's three things. The first thing is falling in love with the subject, and I'm really guilty of this. A client comes to me and says, okay, I want you to write a package about EDTA chelation therapy. I don't know what that is. I've never heard of it before. I start reading the research. As I read it, great salesman always sells himself first. A great salesman is always a great prospect, and I've got the kitschy cabinets full of supplements to prove it. I start reading and I start falling in love with the subject. The next thing you know, I'm going, oh, that is so cool, and I'll write a whole sidebar on something that's really extraneous and kind of takes it just because it's interesting. It takes us down a path that we don't need to go down, and it tends to be confusing. I did this once with horrible results with a 32-page tabloid where I absolutely fell in love with the subject. It was impenetrable. We started out on one thing. We started developing the thing, but then all of a sudden, I started hoping that my market would find this as fascinating as I did, and obviously they did. It bombed big time. A second thing is forgetting the prospect. Once you begin writing about if it's an advertorial, the major trend that you're hitching your wagon to, or if it's a health piece you're talking about the various substances that can address whatever health problem that you're addressing. People tend, younger writers especially, tend to kind of forget about the prospect. It's like, okay, we talked about you, now we're going to talk about me. The point of all of this is all of it needs to come back to the prospect. It drives me crazy when I see copy where it says, vitamin D quells inflammation and reduces risk to the heart. Copy stinks. What you need to be saying is, look buddy, if you're worried about dropping dead instantly of a heart attack because you have an inflamed artery, you need to know that vitamin D is nature's most powerful inflammation soother. That one piece of information can save your life, and I know that because it could save 438,000 people a year who die with healthy arteries. The difference in that is the stilted approach talking about the heart and talking instead about your heart, you, the prospect. And not only that, about how you won't have to worry about this once you take care of making sure you have enough vitamin D. So the writer up front does what you say in bonds with the prospect, but now when it's time to dive in, he has his scientific research on ingredient one, ingredient two, and he's just regurgitating that. That's right, absolutely. Another blunder that writers make, and this is Dan Rosenthal's big complaint, and that is clarity of vision. It kind of ties back to my thing of falling in love with the subject, but you need to create a feeling of momentum in the package. You need to feel like you're actually going someplace with each paragraph. You don't want to be circling back and remaking old points. You need to be feeling like you're moving ahead, and you need to feel like there aren't any side paths here. The sidebars are not going to take you off in a different direction if you have sidebars. They're going to support what you've already said. They can support it with a chart or a figure or a quote, but they support what's said in the main text. That clarity of vision thing is very important, and it's important too when you get down to the offer. In the text, for example, when you're presenting the offer, I only present one offer, and I only present my best one. When they get to the response device, then there's the choices. If they want to cheat out, they can get the one-year offer instead of the two-year offer, but it's going to cost them more per issue, and they're not going to get as many free gifts. So why even mention it in the running text? You mentioned that you need the offer and the sidebars to support clarity of vision to support your main theme. What are some of the sidebars that I should be including in a long piece to support my main idea? I'll just mention 11 of them real quickly, and then we can move on. The first kind of sidebar that I try to include is a biography on the first author of the piece, the person who's supposedly writing this, to demonstrate that he's qualified to have an opinion on this subject, and also that he has a heart for the reader, he's on a crusade, he has compassion for them, he's dedicated his life to solving this problem. Second thing is track record, and again, a financial package that's the trades we've made, how much money we've made, in a health package it could just be testimonials. The next one is little readership, and readership sales throughout, and that would be a pull quote from the text. And quite often I'll write the pull quotes first. What could I say on this spread in a pull quote that would make it absolutely impossible for the reader not to dive into the small copy and read it thoroughly? What could I say here? Usually it's a benefit or it's a threat. It's either when you do this one thing, this will happen, this good thing will happen, or it could be if you're not getting enough vitamin D, this bad thing will happen. And it usually includes a photograph of the author speaking the words. Again, that's a readership sale. Again, you're making the same sale again. Credibility, the next one is. And again, that can be broken down into testimonials. Media mentions about the author that bring him credibility. Appearances, if he was on Nightline or some TV show or in some publication, use that. If he's been quoted in the Wall Street Journal, show a picture of the Wall Street Journal. It's one thing to say, Wall Street Journal calls him all the time for comment, to another to show a picture of the Wall Street Journal. And next to that, and say that Mr. So-and-So's insights and advice frequently appear in the pages of the Wall Street Journal. And the other one is Case History, as we've talked about that before. That's basically a testimonial but written in a narrative. So you're almost living through this. It's like watching an episode of VR. You're actually living through this, vicariously, this particular experience this person had. Process sidebars. This is a biggie. Everyone who takes supplements does it on faith. Show how the thing works. Give me a visual image of how this particular nutrient does the thing it's supposed to do. That can be very, very strong. Another one is Premium Ads. These are simply half-paged, full-page ads that demonstrate the value of the free gift that you're going to give away. This is late in the piece and it really helps to dimensionalize the value of the offer. Product ads. Again, this is an opportunity to pull the benefits product offers out into a sidebar and make them really jump off the page. Offer value sidebars. I know Bob, you remember the old days. I think Agora was who did the most of this, but they would have a math sidebar with a mathematical computation where you added up the value of everything you got. And then at the bottom they would add it all up. You know, it lists each item in it. And total value, you pay, you save. Exactly. Right. And then finally the guarantee. I use a guarantee in three places. I use it in the running text after I've presented my offer. I say it's usually this much, but you only pay this. Plus you get all these things free. And then I have my guarantee copy. On the same spread where I have that guarantee copy, I like to have a sidebar where the guarantee is set out in certificate borders so it looks all nice and official. Or in a very personal sounding letter from the author. Or even something that looks like a contract. We'll talk about that in a minute. The guarantee in a minute. So those are 11 kinds of sidebars. If you didn't get the long article I wrote on this, please let us know and we'll make sure you get a link to it. Well, let's move into that. The guarantee. What do you mean by it should be a contract? The guarantee, a lot of people just do a guarantee that says, hey, you know. Money back guarantee. If you're not satisfied, we turn it in 30 days and we'll refund your money. And that's all it says. That's exactly right. That copy sucks. It's absolutely horrible. First of all, you're raising the specter that they may not be satisfied. That's a major blunder. Right. The guarantee copy should be written. You must be absolutely delighted. Otherwise, then it gives them the option. Instead of saying, hey, you might think this sucks and if it does, we'll give you your money back. You know. So again, remember the guarantee you're selling the product and you're selling the new relationship, it's not simply a risk reliever. A lot of writers sleepwalk through that. It's a major mistake. Secondly, it gives you an opportunity to recite your benefits. This product must do this, must do that, must do the other thing. Otherwise, right. Now your guarantee is gaining some power. Right. I'll also say, you know, your subscription to XYZ plus your free copy of this, your free copy of that, your free copy of this, your free copy of that. You know. All of this stuff must do this or otherwise. Just let me know and I'll rush you a full refund. The other thing on this thing is that when you're writing the guarantee, rushing a refund is important. It's not something like, you know, just let me know and I'll send you your money back. It's important to say that, you know, this is going to happen fast. It's important to give them a specific and say whatever you can. Say give them a performance specific. In the next 12 months, I will do these things for you. And if it's a financial product and it's not a regulated product, if it's a newsletter advisory, not an actual security you're selling, you can say, you know what, I'm going to make you a promise. In the next 12 months, I will put an absolute minimum of $5,000, 50 times what you pay for my newsletter into your brokerage account. If I can't do that, I won't keep your money. You just let me know and I'll rush you a full refund of every penny you pay. And then the line that follows if you're using cliches is that way, comma, you risk nothing. Right. Well, you know, I go on, I basically say you don't have to be a moron not to take an offer like this. Right. And this is a no-brainer. But then the other part of it is the next line really is and even in the unlikely event that this happens, I will absolutely insist that you keep everything I've sent you. Every issue of this, every free gift, enlist them and everything I sent you without any further obligation or cost, they're totally free. So in other words, what does that mean? I will work for an entire year for you and if I don't do what I promise to do that year is yours free. So there's no risk in it for me? None whatsoever. Risk reversal. So what you've done now is you have a contract and the contract can be presented as a contract, you know, you can have your author actually sign it, you know. It can be written as a letter that's very friendly depending on what the product in the subject area is like if it was from a doctor. It can be, you know, here's what I'm concerned about with you, here's what I've committed my life to doing for you and here's what we're going to do together in the next 12 months. And at the end of 12 months, these things will be true that aren't true now. Otherwise, I wouldn't feel right keeping your money. So let me know and I'll send you refund. What I would do, what I'd like to do and this is all of these ideas are general rules that help me write faster and make sure the specific elements I need are in there. But the, you know, on each project you'll see it and say, well, maybe it would work better if I moved this over here or if I did this a little differently. But after I've made my case for why the supplement works or why this major trend in the economy is going to continue and give you opportunities to make lots and lots of money or presented my product if it's a straight product sale. I do the following. First, I repeat, I go back to my main theme. You don't want the back end of your copy. We're talking the last three, four pages of copy here in long copy. You don't want your back end to feel like there's a disconnect from the front end. So I go back to my main theme. If my main headline was give me 90 days and I'll help you, then I have a subhead that says I'm standing by to make the next 90 days the best of your life. And then I'll repeat the benefits that I promised them when I dimensionalized the free gifts they got or the product itself. Then I'll recite the value statements that I gave when I justified the price of the product in the offer section. Then I'll fully dimensionalize that value by comparing it to something of far less import that they're paying more for now. Your old Starbucks trick. My old Starbucks trick. Then I remind them that there's no risk. This is the part I was just talking about where you recite the guarantee. Then I ask for the sale. When asking for the sale, and you have to ask for the sale. You do it repeatedly in the closing. A lot of young writers forget to do this. But you have to tell them now is the moment. You are at a crossroads. We can either go on together or you can go on alone. And here are the things that await you down that path. But if you choose to go with me, here are the things that await you down this path. And you draw that contrast. And you tell them this is the moment. Then you have to tell them specifically what to do. I want you to dial toll free 1-800-blah-blah-blah right now. Or complete and return the enclosed order form in the envelope provided. Or jump on the Internet and log in such and such a place and sign up. Tell them step by step what you want them to do. Then ask for the sale again and remind them of the guarantee. And then repeat your main theme, your main headline and deck theme. Which we have discussed. Repeat that theme. So it is tied in right here at the close. And at this point you are really finishing with a flourish. Make sure your closing line just before the signature ties in with that theme as well. Then you have your signature. And then for the PS, I think I mentioned this last week or the week before, I like adding an urgency premium at the end. This is if I hear from you today or in the next 10 days, depending on whether it is direct mail or Internet, I want to throw in this extra gift that has tremendous value. So that is a great template for closing. Repetition is key when you are asking for the sale and closing your model. And you need to leave them with a feeling. And you need to have a feeling when you read this copy that there is an emotion rising. There is a feeling that I need to do this right now. It is not in 5 minutes. They set this aside and you will never get them back. They have got to do it down. So now we are almost done but they are going now to the order form. Can you give us in our final moments in the end of our 5-Col series, some of the things that I need to do on the order form. And one of them you told me is don't use the word order form in your copy. That is right. Why is that? Because on the one hand there are so many better things you can call it. On the other hand, especially if you have somebody with some dignity presenting your sales message, a doctor, a financial advisor, a scientist, somebody that you want to maintain their credibility and you don't want them to come off sounding like a pitchman, it is so much better if your order form is a free gift certificate or a contract, a better health contract. Enrollment form or something like that. Enrollment form. Just anything but order form. It is too commercial. If you are selling nuts and bolts to a manufacturer of jet airplanes, then an order form is fine. If you are selling a relationship with a customer that is built around a product and a person especially, then you are looking for something that is warmer and more relationship oriented. The other thing is a free gift certificate or something that has to do with value helps you to drive that value concept home. One of the most important things that you do is on the page facing your response device page is you have a visual representation of everything they are going to receive with this offer. If you have five premiums, you show the five premiums twice, you show a fan of the main product, let's say it is a newsletter. This is how we do it with newsletters. Think about this and see how you might do it with other products. With newsletters, we will show a fan of 12 newsletters. Immediately, we have substance. There will be a burst on this that says save 50%. Then there will be a picture of each of the premiums they get with a two year subscription. There could be up to 12 or 14 of them in some cases. Each of those have its own burst that says free or no? Yes. Also, if the newsletter is delivered first class mail, as some are these days, you can say a $29 value free. With the newsletters delivered first class, your headlines on all of your offer copies should reflect the value of the total offer. If they get 10 premiums of $29, they just get $290 worth of free gifts plus they get a $99 discount on their one year subscription. That is $300 worth of discounts and gifts free. If you can use that, it is extremely powerful. Make sure you headline it. Don't let it be an afterthought. Build your offer around that value. Then you show on the same page a one year subscription. Above you show 24 issues and all of the premiums. Then you show the good offer. The first offer is the best offer. The second one is a great offer. For the great offer, you show 12 issues of the newsletters, still a lot of value. You show the five premiums or three premiums or whatever else they are going to get. I also like to show if there is a many of our clients these days will send an easing out every week to their customers or subscribers. I show the easing. Many of them have a website. I show the website. I find a value argument for that. I will say, for example, on a financial product, I will say go to Wall Street, what is it? Jim Cramer's site. Go to street.com and figure out how much it would cost you to subscribe to that. That is the value of the website that you are offering. Other people are charging it and getting it for a financial website. Find a value argument for that. Show everything that they are going to get. Quite often I will look, if they get a weekly easing, if there are daily updates on the homepage of the website, if they get 12 issues in the print newsletter a year, I add that all up. I will say, I am never going to leave you wondering what to do. You are going to hear from me 50, 8, 75, 112 times a year. That is tremendous value. I show that. I will show a computer terminal. I will show an easing. I will show there is a hotline. I will show a telephone hotline or my client speaking on a microphone as if he is doing a teleconference. Dimensionalize the value on that page. Then also on that page, that is my third representation to guarantee stating the order of form is right where it needs to be. Another thing that is important on this spread are testimonials. For a health product, for example, I will use testimonials talking about how easy to order the product was and how friendly the reps were and how fast the product came. Actual testimonials to that effect. If it is a financial newsletter or a health newsletter, I will have testimonials about these wonderful things that have happened in people's lives. Again, credibility at the point of sale. Got it. Unfortunately, we did not get to some of the questions emailed. We will have to discuss how to handle that and let you guys know who are listening. I will get back to you. Can I ask you a question? Yes. Can I put you on the spot? Yes. What are you doing next Wednesday? I will do it. All right. Let us tell you what, guys, if ... No, that is the day before Thanksgiving. I do not know if that is going to work for everybody. It is fine with me, but a lot of people go home early. What do you want to do? I will tell you what. Why do not we do it next Wednesday? For those who cannot join us, we will make the MP3 available. Why do not we say, guys, we will go from 12 to 1 next Wednesday and you get a six call. It will be nothing but wrap up and questions and answers. If you sent me questions, everyone who sent me an email today, we are going to get to it. If you did not, Julie, give the address again. Feedback at makesheaptotalpackage.com. We will meet at noon and we will go for as long as it takes to get the questions. If it takes an hour, we will go to one. There are three questions and there is more than that. We can answer in 15 minutes. That is what we will do. I think it will take an hour just based on what I have already. If you all are going to Thanksgiving dinner and you are getting a call, do not worry about it. We will make the MP3 available to you. I just want to make sure we fulfill on what we promised you. Terrific idea. Everyone have a great Thanksgiving. Yes, and we will see a lot of you next Wednesday. All right. Thanks, guys. Bye-bye. Bye-bye. Bye-bye.
